speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything-- before we go on to Theresienstadt, is there anything that you want to say about your relationship with your father, and how it helped you to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was, on that part, exceedingly lucky. Not only did I survive with my father, but all along together. Together, meaning side-by-side. This must be considered an exceptional case of luck. My father and I were always very close, and were always giving to one another, not only food-- which meant life at that time-- but also moral support, advice, shelter, all those things that helped in survival. We had a reason, at least from Buchenwald on-- after Buchenwald, Schlieben. And so we had very good reason to believe that we may actually survive. What was on our mind was Ida. She was split off from us after [NON-ENGLISH]. She went to-- to a camp in Leipzig. Of course, thank heaven, she too survived. But we were concerned, and we didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you go to Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Theresienstadt at the end of the war, in apparently March, 1945. This was a time when the Germans, they hardly knew what to do with these prisoners. But the killing had stopped. So others that we know now were walking in long marches. But we were taken into cattle cars, and we went on a long journey to-- to Theresienstadt. The journey was of great interest. We were not 100 in a cattle car. We were more like 60 in a cattle car. But the doors were now open. And at any stop, we could run out into the fields and find something edible like turnips or so. The SS was now inside the car, and we came to the days of glory when we saw them ripping off their own insignia, trying to hide their identity. They also acquired our lice, and were now lousing themselves together with us. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The feeding that we received on the road was very slim. But again, we were now-- we were very weakened physically, hardly able to do anything. But we were free to run into the fields and get something that sustained us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bruno, why did you jump back into the train? Why not just keep running?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where will you go? There is nowhere to go. You are in Germany. They're all Germans. And you-- we-- our problem, at this point, was not murder. Our problem at this point was food for survival. In any event, by the time-- it took quite a few days, I can't tell how many. But it took quite a few days-- it was a very slow journey-- to get to Theresienstadt. In Theresienstadt, they were organized. We were taken to the [NON-ENGLISH]. I revisited-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] barracks. There were military barracks. Hamburger-- they called it Hamburger, after the town of Hamburg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they had some sanitary facilities, and better sleeping facilities, nothing like the Ritz Hotel, but-- but by comparison. And we felt like we were not exposed to brutality. And we were not. Even though it turned out later, from documents, that we were, at one time, meant to be done-- to-- to be executed, to be liquidated. But it never came to that. And then one day, the Russians came in. Now the Russians were very kind to us. The first thing they did is gave us medical attention, some better sanitary facilities, and food in large amounts. Maybe too much, because the Russians had apparently cashed in on German supplies. All those supplies that they gave us were German. But they were very generous with us. Many of those, the tank commanders that came in, were Jewish boys from Moscow, even from-- Polish boys that had escaped to Russia and joined the army. And the commander of the tank asked me-- I walked out of the camp. I could hardly walk, I was so weakened. I weighed about 90 pounds. But the commander of the tank found me in the street and was wondering where I'm going. And I said, I would like to take revenge on the Germans. He said, no. You sit here on the tank. He put a pillow on his tank. It was a beautiful summer day. I mean, it was May. And he gave-- he served food to me, anything I wanted, really. He said, I will take the revenge. And there was a lot of that. It was a delightful day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to be more specific about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We paid them back with the same coin. Those-- I told one officer, who was squashed by his tank against a wall-- he was a tall, handsome, German, SS officer, who was very-- begging for his life, very cowardly. I told him, how can a big fellow like you be so ruthless a killer, and so cowardly when-- when your time comes to pay? I say, did you really think you must never pay for it? It went on and on. We had some nice times. The Russian army was then under the spirit of their minister of propaganda, Ilya Ehrenburg. Ilya Ehrenburg was Jewish. He was a minister of propaganda for Stalin. What Goebels was to Hitler, Ehrenburg was to Stalin. And he encouraged revenge, and rightly so. This kind of a heinous crime that has been perpetrated-- not only to Jews-- Jews and gypsies, people of eastern extraction, like the Polish or the Russians. This brutality, this inhuman behavior had to be punished. This was the time for it. As I recovered, I sort of joined with the Russian army. I was basically a medic. Little did I know that this would later be my vocation. But-- but-- I-- at that time, they simply thought I'd be good to put on bandages and stuff. And I did that for the Russians, and I lived with them. I ate with them. I picked up their language. And as for as I'm concerned, they were life savers, certainly to me. Without them, I wouldn't be around. But this ended. And I went to Prague. And there, I-- I was a money exchanger for a while. I was then-- because of-- of-- of my lingual skills, I conversed in a number of languages that were-- that were desirable at-- at that period of time and in this place. People were traveling to Poland, to Russia, to Germany, to France. And there was a need for money exchange. I used to say, I am the main banker in Prague. In fact, I was in contact with the Narodni Bank.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak Czech, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. Very little. But if you speak Russian and Polish, you can understand a good deal. And the basics, I knew. But when I was last in Czechoslovakia, two years ago or so, and I read the paper and the signs, I could understand nothing. And all the people told me, you've got to understand Czech if you speak Polish. The fact is, no. It's-- it's very much like the Russian and the Polack-- they are related languages, but if they want to converse with one another, they need an interpreter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me. You were changing money. And you were speaking the languages. You were comfortable. What about your father? Was he still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was with me all the time. And after-- after we made some money in that money exchange business, we took the opportunity to go to the west, and-- with the idea, of course, as always, to go to Palestine. There was no Israel. It was a Palestine thing. But it was already the Jewish land that we dreamt of. And for that reason, we left and went to Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you fairly healthy by then? Had you regained your weight?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had regained. I'll show it to you in a picture. I had regained by then. The-- the regaining-- if you survive-- now I'm talking as a physician-- if you survive the initial transit period between starvation and-- and rehydration-- particularly rehydration-- and-- and feeding, there is a transition period there where you have to readjust the system. But once you survive this, the rest is a rapid progress. It is much easier to gain weight than it is to lose. You'll find that out. [LAUGHS] I did, already. [LAUGHS] Yes. And young people that regain weight and strength, reinvigorate. I was reinvigorated-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] three months or four months after.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father was a strong man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he wasn't all that old. How old were you when the war ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was born in 1923. The war ended in 1945. I was 22 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you went to Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Munich. And in Munich, we engaged in business. We started-- we started in a number of enterprises-- import/export, and-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we were into scrap metals. We were into a number of businesses. And we succeeded fairly well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't you go on to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you do know that entering Palestine was very difficult. Of course, after 1948, there was the establishment of the State of Israel. But by then, we were already geared toward America where we had family ties. And so in March of 1951, I came to America. By that time, I spoke fluent English, better than today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school in Munich at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school in Munich at that time. But I returned after I initially came here to study medicine.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And then when you came here, where did you go at first?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came here, I was in medical training.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chicago in the beginning, yes. I was at Chicago Medical School. And then in Arizona, at the Maricopa County General Hospital in Phoenix, and in Tucson at the Medical Center. And later, I finished residency at the VA Hospital in Long Beach. And there, we were ranked militarily. Later, I-- I was an officer at the draft board.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- you picked boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: I fought the war in Vietnam [LAUGHS] at its very inception [LAUGHS] in the draft board.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I married, and I was married. I married in 1969. And I was married for eight years.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there were no children?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no children, and it led to divorce. This was the reason. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are no longer in practice?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I worked until I was 68 years old. And in 1970, I retired. '70? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Let me see. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was-- I believe it was 1970. 1990, I mean, for God's sakes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. OK. Bruno, you've-- you've written a great deal, and thought a great deal about religion, and politics, and philosophy. What would you like to say to future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot. The first thing you want to say to generations is to remember that humanity is cruel. And you survive by being strong and able to defend yourselves. That is particularly pertinent today. But we are establishing peace in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: There is an old Hebrew saying, [HEBREW]-- If I won't do it for myself, who will do it for me? In any event, we need a youth that it is capable in every way-- intellectually, professionally trained, up to date in technology and so, and able to fend for itself, to defend themselves if any turbulence comes about. We-- we had problems along this line. We get questions all the time-- how come you went like sheep to the slaughter? And the answer is, we were religiously backward, primitive people who actually believed that God will save us. It didn't turn out to be that way. If we organized and put up more resistance, not more people would have survived. No. But we would have died differently.

speaker: interviewee
text: And most of all, our killers would have died with us. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There is a difference of how one dies. In any event, I won't engage into religious dissertation here. But in my writings and so, while I am definitely for synagogue and perpetuation of our culture-- that is, in large part, beautiful-- of our traditions that we inherited and are part of, our religion, in my-- in my little world, should survive, but not be taken too seriously. Because much of it is myth, as you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I won't go any further on that subject. So your feeling is that humanity is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Humanity is a beast. It is a-- is an animal. It is a-- it is a beast, actually. It is the cruelest of all animals. And if you want to live in the world, you must be prepared to-- well let's quote these things-- the fittest survive. And-- and you must be fit for survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you made yourself fit by becoming a doctor? How else did you become fit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I really don't count. If you don't produce a family, if there's no descendants, then it's-- it's immaterial. It becomes irrelevant what happens to me. After all, I'm 72, and I cannot die young. [LAUGHS] But for those in my family, we are blessed with a lot of children and descendants. And to those, I advise. I don't like to see any Jewish home without a weapon, even if it's only symbolic. There should never be a day when somebody comes after your children who has a pistol, and you have none. I keep a weapon here, a beautiful one. I never used it. It's symbolic. But it must be here. It's more important than the [NON-ENGLISH]. Just in case.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, very much. I'd like to be able to show your pictures now.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandmother, the maternal part. Her name was Chava Mindel. She came from the house of [NON-ENGLISH]. And this picture was done under the German occupation in the ghetto for German identification. It was a very poor picture that I kind of revived, photographically-speaking.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is the lady whom you told the story about?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the lady that was clubbed to death, yes. My maternal grandfather, Nechemia Bieder, who was one of the most distinguished and beloved men in town. He appears here on a picture taken in the ghetto during the German occupation, and which this picture was taken without a head covering, because of German instructions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Gorlice in about 1941. These are my parents. They were married in 1920. That is a wedding picture taken in Breslau, where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Laura Bieder, And my father's name, Kalman Zwass. This is a family picture taken before the war, when we were young-- that is to say, the children. You see my mother, my father, my elder brother, Joseph, and myself. The picture was done in Breslau where we lived. And the year would probably be 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you give me the name of your parents please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Laura Bieder. And my father's name, Kalman Zwass.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are Bruno?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Bruno, and my brother was Joseph. This is a picture taken about four months after liberation in Czechoslovakia, in Prague. I wear a German uniform. At the time, I thought I'm very elegant. That is the best I could pick. Next to me is Ruschka and Heniek. They are now married. We befriended them in Prague after liberation. They too were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: You forgot your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name remains Bruno Zwass. I'm Bruno Zwass. In the middle of this picture, I am sort of a professor here in Tucson. I had the privilege to train doctors for the University of New Mexico City. The group behind me are boys from the Medical School of Mexico City, some of whom turned out to be great. Behind me, the boy is Oscar Litvak, who is a prominent gynecologist now. On the very left, is Gesundteit, who became a professor at Stanford, or someplace. The guy next to him became a psychiatrist. He had the good fortune to have an uncle who became president of Mexico. And now he is in charge of population control in Mexico, which I consider to be the highest office in the nation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you told him so, didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Oh, yes. We are good friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Juan Luis Caillu Alvarez. He was related to Luis at [NON-ENGLISH], when he was president and now, to-- Thank you.
